item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like the effect of hearing the tape should be described differently, based on the test logs. The fact that the description specifically mentions the tone of voice not affecting the personality overwrite really threw me off when I read the drunk test log. Maybe 'state of mind' would work better than personality?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wanted to point out that this isn't tagged as eric, but on reflection, it doesn't feel like an Eric skip, and the name seemed to have been added as an afterthought anyway, judging by the early comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wanted to up vote, but it just doesn't grab me. sorry.

item.title: New photo
item.content:encoded:
Got a new photo.

Image is by flickr user edvvc. It was found here and distributed under a Creative Commons Attribution 2.0 Generic license.

item.title: Image Attribution
item.content:encoded:
Original pic is from here (All rights Reserved (c) 2010 by Eskwadrat). Specifically, this image. So, if Eskwadrat wants this gone, all they need to do is ask.

New image. See below.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it okay… but… There's something about the effect that seems a little incoherent. Maybe that's the intention, but the confusion is taking away from my enjoyment rather than adding to it.

Eh, what the hell. +1. Squeaked under the wire anyhow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting, though it could really use an extended experiment log page. That could be…interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What the hell is this Eric kid doing to his electronics any? At this rate he'll get listed as a scip pretty soon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For the first test, 'glitch' is probably an accurate description of what was going on — however it was "designed" to only use human voices, it accidentally left an exception for dogs.

And I'd counter that alcohol most certainly alters your personality, if only temporarily. 1248 simply takes a snapshot of one's personality at that moment in time.

While I'm here: Somewhere there are six guys who all act just like Ozzy Osbourne.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The first two tests seem kind of glitchy when you consider the SCP's description…

A dog barking does not qualify as a "human voice," which is one of the prerequisites for the anomalous doohickey to do its thing. Shouldn't the guy have been completely unaffected? Or if there were human voices on the recording as well, shouldn't one of their personalities gotten grafted on?

As for the second one, being drunk doesn't really seem to qualify as a personality trait. It's a chemical adjustment, like hallucinating on LSD or being numb while injected with novocaine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, thanks for catching that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I fixed your test log.

You had D-2344
Then you had in a later test D-2348, but referred to them as D-2344 twice.

Hope it isn't a problem that I made that tweak.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Then why did the recording of the dog affect the D-class?

Because that was the first animal test they did, and didn't feel the need to edit the main description to reflect it, I suppose. The OOC reasoning is that I think it flows nicer than anything that would specify that non-human vocalizations would do stuff too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1248’s anomalous properties manifest when a person listens to an instance of SCP-1248-1 with one or more human voices audible

Then why did the recording of the dog affect the D-class? More importantly, why did the silence affect a D-class?

Still upvoting, because while pointing this out, I got Fridge Horror about the silence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It was a quite decent SCP, until this line.

D-5552 showed signs of fear and expressed discomfort with being tangible.

At this point, it became utterly wonderful for me - upvoted after my giggle-fits stopped.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I think of one, I'll put it in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I bet there are better ways to become non-solid without setting yourself on fire. That's the part that bothers me more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It looks like gratuitous "creepy thing what makes you crazy and kill yourself, woooo" writing.

It might come off as that, but the intent was to imply that the personality of silence/static/whatever has a strong compulsion to stay non-tangible, if that makes any sense. I mean, if there was another way to become non-solid without killing yourself, I would have used that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, except the last test. It looks like gratuitous "creepy thing what makes you crazy and kill yourself, woooo" writing.

Most problem I have with it is that, if absolute silence has a "personality", it should manifest in any test randomly. Also, tape recorders never have absolute silence, that would be the personality of random magnetic static, but I guess I'm just nitpicking by now. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It would probably work for a while, then it would roar. Everyone who hears the roar would then have 682's personality, forever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It'd probably go over about as well as that test with the one paddle that changes your attitude. Or worse, because it's 682 and it makes everything worse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder what would happen if someone tried to use this to shift 682's demeanor. Hm…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eric's stuff:

  • SCP-1248
  • SCP-168
  • SCP-066
  • SCP-683
  • (Not really, but for bonus points: SCP-335-Disk 148)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just added the Eric reference to this one a few weeks ago, after it had been written for a while. It felt fitting for some reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, wow. Didn't notice that. Hell, that's practically a GoI already. At least three articles about the items of a little kid named Eric, whose mother (?) is rather… doting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And… if SCP-066 were to be looking for Eric instead of Joey…

…I *like* it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
DID YOU MISPLACE YOUR IPAD AGAIN, ERIC?

such a forgetful boy…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm here, but where the hell is my iPad?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
ERIC

WHERE IS ERIC

You know, SCP-683 is also Eric-themed.

And… if SCP-066 were to be looking for Eric instead of Joey…

Hmm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is there some link between this and SCP-168? They're much to alike to be coincidental.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think he means a catatonic state, actually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ooh, good question. I think I'll add that in.

EDIT: I cannot believe I forgot to add tags.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if someone listens to a recording played backwards?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd imagine nothing would happen in both situations;
Putting Bright into someone with the personality of Bright would result in Dr Bright taking over, regardless of mental status or even species.

And being a vegetable means they're brain dead. Wouldn't matter what personality you tried to give them, they'd still essentially be brain dead. Only thing that'd work is Bright's necklace or one of the Cure-all pills. And they are not giving up a cure all pill for that.

It's a personality shift, not a complete transfer/conversion of memories.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) Get Bright to record his voice on this one.
2) Get someone to listen to that recording.
3) Make that someone wear SCP-963.

Also, what if someone in a vegetable condition listened to a recording of a healthy and sane person?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hm… you know what, I agree. Plus, I've always wanted to end an SCP on "attempted to incinerate himself".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd get rid of the murmuring. The personality shift seems reasonable and neat, but the murmuring is just sort of "this SCP has seeeeecret super dangerous powers that are being hinted at". Which works a decent amount of the time, but I don't think is necessary here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I imagine that they would retain the same personality but would still be susceptible to being altered by 1248. I think I'll put in a thing about that.

Also, I'm going to use this edit to try and address Mackenzie's concern up there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What happens if someone listens to a recording of their own voice? Does nothing happen or do their personality features become exaggerated or what?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I put it there mainly to suggest that absolute silence had a personality and a voice, and the exact nature of that didn't particularly interest me. However, you do make a valid point that it would be useful to have something there. Any ideas?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last test entry bugs me; it doesn't hint at what the possible result was strongly enough to justify its existence, and just feels tacked on as a kind of scare tactic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. That would certainly be problematic, but then again, a lot of the SCPs could be dangerous in the wrong hands. we'll just have to hope that doesn't happen.
  2. Also something that would interest them, but it didn't feel interesting enough to put in. I'd personally guess that it doesn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A couple of things:

  1. If I were the Foundation, I'd be worried about a spy loyal to another GoI making a recording on this thing and then playing it over the facility's PA system, or the same thing happening if the facility was invaded by a GoI. Or a spy simply making a recording, smuggling it out, and using it brainwash people outside the facility.
  2. If I were the Foundation, I'd want to know if SCP-1248-1 still has anomalous effects if broadcast over radio/cable/Internet/etc.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It would certainly make an interesting tale, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this.

Just to note, keep Gears, or really any major researcher, away from this. Don't need any of them to have a sudden personality shift.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll make this more clear.

EDIT: Done. Mentioned in the last paragraph of the description.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's unclear to me whether SCP-1248 changes personalities based on the actual person whose voice is recorded or the sound of their voice, which takes away from the article. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one's just been bouncing around in my head for a while. Enjoy.
